elements.sent-2411-001: The problem of interfacing quantum mechanics and gravity has long been an unresolved issue in physics.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recent advances in precision measurement technology suggest that detecting gravitational effects in massive quantum systems, particularly gravity-induced entanglement (GIE) in the oscillator system, could provide crucial empirical evidence for revealing the quantum nature of the gravitational field.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, thermal decoherence imposes strict constraints on system parameters.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, the inequality $2 \gamma_m k_B T < \hbar G \Lambda \rho$ limits the relationship between mechanical dissipation $\gamma_m$, effective temperature $T$, oscillator density $\rho$, form factor $\Lambda$ and fundamental constants.
elements.sent-2411-005: This inequality, based on the GIE's inherent property of noise model, is considered universally across experimental systems and cannot be improved by quantum control.
elements.sent-2411-006: Given the challenges in further optimizing $\gamma_m$, $\rho$, and $T$ near their limits, optimizing the form factor $\Lambda$ may reduce demands on other parameters.
elements.sent-2411-007: This work examines the optimal geometry that maximizes the form factor $\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-008: It is mathematically proven that the form factor $\Lambda$, which depends on the geometry and spatial arrangement of the oscillators, has a supremum of $2\pi$ in all geometries and spatial arrangements.
elements.sent-2411-009: This result improves by about one order of magnitude over two spherical oscillators.
elements.sent-2411-010: This establishes a fundamental limit, aiding future experiments and quantum gravitational detection efforts.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, to this day, no empirical evidence of the quantum gravity effect has been observed.
elements.sent-2411-012: In recent years, advances in precision measurement technology have raised the possibility of detecting of gravitational effects within massive quantum systems at lower energies in the foreseeable future \cite{Santos2017, Li2018, Westphal2021, Whittle2021, Liu2021, Youssefi2023, Fuchs2024, Bose2023, Carney2019, Gallerati2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Specifically, detecting gravity-induced entanglement (GIE) in these systems is expected to provide crucial empirical evidence for the quantum nature of the gravitational field \cite{Feynman1957, Bose2017, Bose2022, Marshman2020, Marletto2017, Miao2020, Plato2023, Kafri2014, Krisnanda2020, Qvarfort2020, Cosco2021, Weiss2021, Balushi2018, Matsumura2020, Biswas2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Among these approaches, GIE-based experiments in quantum mechanical oscillator systems entangled through central-potential interactions are among the primary candidate methods \cite{Kafri2014, Krisnanda2020, Miao2020, Qvarfort2020, Plato2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Various of experimental schemes have been proposed for such studies.
elements.sent-2411-016: However, a common challenge in these proposals is that thermal decoherence can destroy the GIE, imposing strict constraints on the system parameters necessary for the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-017: Across proposals like two free oscillators \cite{Kafri2014 , linearized optomechanics \cite{Miao2020 , levitated nano-systems \cite{Rijavec2021 , and modulated optomechanics \cite{Plato2023 , requirements align with the same inequality \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} 2 \gamma_m k_B T < \hbar G \Lambda \rho \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\gamma_m$ is mechanical dissipation of the oscillator, $T$ is the system effective temperature, $\rho$ oscillator density, and $\Lambda$ is a form factor related to the geometry and spatial arrangements of the two oscillators.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: This universal inequality cannot be optimized through local quantum control methods \cite{Miao2020, Plato2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: This inequality reveals several significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-020: First, it involves only four system parameters $\gamma_m$, $T$, $\rho$, and $\Lambda$, which means that, for optimization, there is limited room for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-021: The ability to fine-tune the system is constrained to these four parameters alone, leaving little flexibility for optimizing additional system parameters in order to satisfy the inequality.
elements.sent-2411-022: Furthermore, when system parameters reach certain thresholds, further optimization becomes prohibitively costly.
elements.sent-2411-023: For instance, in large-mass mechanical oscillators, ground-state cooling introduces a theoretical limit to the effective temperature, and at ultra-low temperatures, improvements in parameters like density $\rho$ and dissipation $\gamma_m$ are constrained by the intrinsic material properties of the oscillator, with little room for further enhancement through external means (e.g., applying higher prestress in membrane-based systems).
elements.sent-2411-024: Furthermore, the maximum possible density, $\rho$, is limited by the densest known material, osmium, which has a density of approximately \SI{22.4 {\gram\per\cubic\centi\metre .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: This further restricts the upper bound on $\rho$, making it another challenge to optimize the system.
elements.sent-2411-026: Given these difficulties, one might naturally consider whether improvements in the geometry and spatial arrangement of the oscillators could increase the value of the form factor $\Lambda$, thereby indirectly reducing the need for optimization of the other parameters.
elements.sent-2411-027: In a previous work, the case of two spherical oscillators was considered, for which it was shown that the form factor $\Lambda$ is bounded above by $\pi/3$ \cite{Krisnanda2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: In contrast, for two oscillators arranged in a cylindrical configuration, the form factor can reach a value of as high as $2.0$ \cite{Miao2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: This naturally raises the question of whether other geometries and spatial arrangements could yield a higher value of $\Lambda$, and whether there exists a theoretical upper bound for $\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this paper, a rigorous mathematical proof is provided that the supremum of $\Lambda$ over all possible geometries and spatial arrangements is $2\pi$, thus resolving this question substantially.
elements.sent-2411-031: The form factor in oscillator-based GIE experiments derives from the gravitational interaction term in the Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-032: For oscillators made of two identical masses $M$ with uniform density $\rho$ and arbitrary configuration – whether on a membrane \cite{Liu2021 , suspended by a pendulum \cite{Miao2020 , or as levitated nano-systems \cite{Rijavec2021 – the low-energy gravitational interaction Hamiltonian is described by their Newtonian potential \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} H_G = V_G(x_1,x_2) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where displacements $x_1$ and $x_2$ from equilibrium are considered small perturbations, allowing expansion of $V_G(x_1,x_2)$ in terms of $x_1 - x_2$, namely \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} V_G(x_1,x_2) = V|_{(0,0)} + \partial_{x_1 -x_2}V|_{(0,0)}(x_1 -x_2) + \frac{1}{2}\partial^2_{x_1 -x_2}V|_{(0,0)}(x_1 -x_2)^2 + o[(x_1 -x_2)^2]. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Retaining terms up to the second order, and since the system is at equilibrium, the zeroth-order and first-order terms can be neglected, one then obtains \cite{Schmole2016 \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} H_G(x_1,x_2) = \frac{1}{2}\partial^2_{x_1 -x_2}V|_{(0,0)}(x_1 -x_2)^2. \end{aligned}\end{equation} It can be seen that the magnitude of $\partial^2_{x_1 -x_2 V|_{(0,0)}$ can be expressed as \begin{equation}\label{eq:rhoGLambda} \begin{aligned} \left|\frac{1}{2}\partial^2_{x_1 -x_2}V|_{(0,0)}\right| = \frac{1}{2}G M \Lambda \rho \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\Lambda$ is a dimensionless and scale-invariant quantity known as the form factor that depends solely on the geometry, spatial arrangements and direction of oscillation of the oscillators \cite{Krisnanda2020, Miao2020 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: To express it explicitly, one can assume that the two masses occupy non-overlapping domains $A$ and $B$, in which case their gravitational potential is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:VG} \begin{aligned} V_G (A,B) = -G\rho^2 \int_A \int_B \frac{1}{|r-r'|}\mathrm{d}^3r \mathrm{d}^3r'. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Assume that the two oscillators are placed relative to each other such that the relative displacement of the two masses is always parallel to a fixed direction represented by a unit vector $n$.
elements.sent-2411-034: Differentiate Eq. \ref{eq:VG and compare it with Eq. \ref{eq:rhoGLambda , one obtains \begin{equation}\label{eq:Lambda} \begin{aligned} \Lambda (A,B,n) = \left|\frac{1}{V}\int_A \int_B n\cdot \nabla_{r}(n\cdot \nabla_{r'}\frac{1}{|r - r'|})\mathrm{d}^3r\mathrm{d}^3r'\right| \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $V$ is the volume of the masses $A$ or $B$.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The value of $\Lambda$ depends both on the geometry and spatial arrangement of $A$ and $B$, as well as the direction of oscillation $n$.
elements.sent-2411-036: It can be interpreted as the rate of change of the gravitational force (i.e., tidal force) between two domains $A$ and $B$, each with unit density and unit volume, in the direction of $n$.
elements.sent-2411-037: In the quantum scenario, $x_1$ and $x_2$ can be replaced by the quantized particle positions $\hat{x _1$ and $\hat{x _2$ namely \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \hat{H}_G = \frac{1}{2}\partial^2_{x_1 -x_2}V|_{(0,0)} (\hat{x}_1 - \hat{x}_2). \end{aligned}\end{equation} In order for quantum entanglement to persist despite thermal decoherence, the interaction rate must exceed the decoherence rate, which yields \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \frac{||\hat{H}_G||}{\hbar} > \frac{2 M \gamma_m k_B T \delta x_q^2}{\hbar} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\delta x_q$ a characteristic length scale given by \cite{Miao2020 and \cite{Braginsky1995 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: With the expression for $\Lambda$ in Eq. \ref{eq:rhoGLambda , this gives \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} 2\gamma_m k_B T < \hbar G \Lambda \rho. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In existing studies, the form factor for specific geometries and spatial arrangements has been investigated.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In \cite{Krisnanda2020 , the form factor for two spherical oscillators was studied, yielding \( \Lambda < \pi/3 \).}
elements.sent-2411-040: In \cite{Miao2020 , the form factor for cylinders with different radius, heights and separation along the axis was studied, reaching a value of approximately 2.0.}
elements.sent-2411-041: To motivate the main result, an intuition is first provided as to why the geometry of two spherical oscillators is not the optimal choice.
elements.sent-2411-042: Without loss of generality, let $n = e_z$ be the unit vector along $z$-axis, then the kernel function of $\Lambda$, given by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} e_z\cdot \nabla_{r}(e_z\cdot \nabla_{r'}\frac{1}{|r - r'|}) = \frac{2(z-z')^2 - (x-x')^2 - (y-y')^2}{|r-r'|^5} \end{aligned}\end{equation} has different signs in different directions.
elements.sent-2411-043: In general, these terms tend to cancel each other, resulting in a reduction in the value of $\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-044: To reduce cancellation between positive and negative contributions, the oscillators should be extended objects along the $z$-axis or the $xy$-plane alone.
elements.sent-2411-045: However, the former idea does not work better because the average distance between the mass elements is too large, making the tidal force weak.
elements.sent-2411-046: A toy model is now provided to illustrate how extended objects along the $xy$-plane can increase the form factor.
elements.sent-2411-047: In the $xy$-plane, a ball with radius $1/2$ is placed at every point $(i,j)$, for $i,j \in \mathbb{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-048: If $i+j$ is even, the object is labeled $A$; if $i+j$ is odd, it is labeled $B$.
elements.sent-2411-049: Thus, $B$ exerts a tidal force on the ball at $(0,0)$ with the value \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} |\partial_z F_z|=\sum_{i+j \text{ is odd}}\frac{G(\frac{4}{3}\pi (\frac{1}{2})^3\rho)^2}{(i^2+j^2)^{3/2}}\approx 5.84 G(\frac{4}{3}\pi (\frac{1}{2})^3\rho)^2. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In real situation, the lattice of balls is large but finite, the number of balls each type is denoted by $N$, then $V=N\frac{4 {3}\pi (\frac{1 {2 )^3$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Ignoring the edge effect when $N$ is large, the tidal force between $A$ and $B$ is equal to $5.84 NG((4/3)\pi (1/2)^3\rho)^2$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Therefore, the form factor is $\Lambda\approx 5.84\pi/6$, which is approximately equal to 3.06.
elements.sent-2411-052: In this toy model, balls are used for the analytic calculation, but due to the gaps between them, this geometry is not considered optimal, as smaller balls could be placed in the gaps to further increase $\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Since the packing factor of the above lattice is $\pi/6 \approx 0.52$, it can be inferred that, in the absence of gaps, the form factor $\Lambda$ can be approximately doubled, reaching a value around 6.
elements.sent-2411-054: This section discusses the optimal geometry that maximizes the form factor.
elements.sent-2411-055: As the main result, the supremum of the possible values of the form factor is provided for all geometries, spatial arrangements, and oscillation directions.
elements.sent-2411-056: To ensure the physical validity of the subsequent discussion, some additional constraints must be imposed on the domains $A$ and $B$ under consideration.
elements.sent-2411-057: First, $A$ and $B$ must be bounded, as it is physically impossible to create unbounded objects.
elements.sent-2411-058: In addition, $A$ and $B$ must be separated, so that they can be each identified as distinct objects.
elements.sent-2411-059: Based on these considerations, the following theorem is obtained: \begin{theo Let $\mathcal{S}$ be the set of pairs $(A, B)$ of three-dimensional bounded domains $A$ and $B$ of equal volume $V$, where $A$ and $B$ are separated.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Denote by $S^2$ the unit sphere of all unit vectors $n$, then \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \sup_\mathcal{S} \max_{S^2} \Lambda (A,B,n) = 2\pi. \end{aligned}\end{equation} \end{theo To prove Theorem 1, the key is to show that $\sup_\mathcal{S \max_{S^2 \Lambda (A,B,n)$ are both $\leq 2\pi$ and $\geq 2\pi$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: They can each be demonstrated through the following two lemmas, the complete proofs of which are provided in Appendices \ref{subsec:proof_lemma2 and \ref{subsec:proof_lemma1 .}}

elements.sent-2411-068: This is the following lemma: Lemma 2 For any $\epsilon >0$, there exist a pair $(A,B)$ and a direction unit vector $n$ such that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} |\Lambda (A, B, n) - 2\pi|<\epsilon. \end{aligned}\end{equation} This completes the proof of Theorem 1.
elements.sent-2411-069: The gravity-induced entanglement (GIE) experiment has recently been considered a strong candidate for testing the quantum nature of gravitational fields.
elements.sent-2411-070: However, one of the significant challenges faced by such experiments is the effects of thermal decoherence in the system.
elements.sent-2411-071: This imposes stringent constraints on the system parameters involved in the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-072: In GIE experiments based on oscillators, this constraint is summarized by the inequality $2\gamma_m k_B T < \hbar G \Lambda \rho$, where $\gamma_m$ is the mechanical dissipation of the oscillator, $T$ is the effective temperature of the system, $\rho$ is the density of the oscillator, and $\Lambda$ is the form factor.
elements.sent-2411-073: This inequality, arising from the inherent property of noise model of the GIE, is considered to be universally applicable across diverse experimental systems and cannot be improved by other quantum control methods.
elements.sent-2411-074: In this paper, the supremum of the form factor $\Lambda$ for the oscillator is obtained in all geometries and spatial arrangements of the GIE experiment based on the quantum oscillator system.
elements.sent-2411-075: This result provides a fundamental limit for the optimization of system parameters in the future GIE experiments.
elements.sent-2411-076: Compared to the case of the spherical oscillator (for which $\Lambda < \pi/3$), this supremum increases by nearly an order of magnitude, making it particularly important when other system parameters, namely $\gamma_m$, $T$, and $\rho$, are optimized to their limits.. .

elements.sent-2411-081: To prove Lemma 2, it can first be observed that the following lemma Lemma 2.1 For any $x_1,y_i,z_i,x'_1,y'_i,z'_i\geq 0$ where $i=+,-$, the following sixfold parametrized definite integral can be analytically evaluated \begin{equation}\label{eq:MainIntegral} \begin{aligned} I(x_+,x_-,y_+,y_-,z_+,z_-;x'_+,x'_-,y'_+,y'_-,z'_+,z'_-) = \int_{x_-}^{x_+}\mathrm{d}x \int_{x'_-}^{x'_+}\mathrm{d}x' \int_{y_-}^{y_+}\mathrm{d}y \int_{y'_-}^{y'_+}\mathrm{d}y' \int_{z_-}^{z_+}\mathrm{d}z \int_{z'_-}^{z'_+}\mathrm{d}z' \frac{2(z-z')^2 - (x-x')^2 - (y-y')^2}{[(x-x')^2 + (y-y')^2 + (z-z')^2]^{5/2}} =\sum_{i,i',j,j',k,k'\in \{+,-\}}(-1)^{i}(-1)^{i'}(-1)^{j}(-1)^{j'}(-1)^{k}(-1)^{k'} F(x_{i}-x_{i'}',y_{j}-y_{j'}',z_{k}-z_{k'}') \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} F(x,y,z) = \frac{2z^2 - x^2 - y^2}{6}\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} +\frac{1}{4}x(y^2-3z^2)\tanh^{-1}\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}} +\frac{1}{4}y(x^2-3z^2)\tanh^{-1}\frac{y}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}} -xyz\tan^{-1}\frac{xy}{z\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} and $(-1)^\pm = \pm 1$.

elements.sent-2411-083: Redefine the oscillation direction as $n = e_z$, and consider the following class of pairs $(A, B)$, which are parameterized by three parameters $H, h >0$ and $N \in \mathbb{N ^+$, and defined as interiors of the following sets \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \overline{A(H,h,N)} = \{(x,y,z)\in \mathbb{R}^3| \frac{2i-2}{2N}\leq x\leq \frac{2i-1}{2N} \text{for some $i=1,...,n$}, 0\leq y\leq 1, 0\leq z\leq H\} \cup \{(x,y,z)\in \mathbb{R}^3| 0\leq x,y\leq 1, -h \leq z\leq 0\} \end{aligned}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \overline{B(H,h,N)} = \{(x,y,z)\in \mathbb{R}^3| \frac{2j-1}{2N}\leq x\leq \frac{2j}{2N} \text{for some $j=1,...,n$}, 0\leq y\leq 1, 0\leq z\leq H\} \cup \{(x,y,z)\in \mathbb{R}^3| 0\leq x,y\leq 1, H \leq z\leq H+h\}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Then, for any $H, h>0$ and $N \in \mathbb{N ^+$, one could express the form factor $\Lambda$ of $A(H,h,N)$ and $B(H,h,N)$ and direction $n =e_z$ in terms of the integral $I$ in Lemma 2.1, given by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N),e_z) = |\frac{2}{H+2h}(I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + I_4)| \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} I_1 =\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0), I_2 =\sum_{i}^{N}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H+h,H), I_3 =\sum_{j}^{N}I(\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,0,-h), I_4 = I(1,0,1,0,H+h,H;1,0,1,0,0,-h). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Firstly, fixing any $H$ and $N$, one finds that as $h\to 0$, $I_2, I_3, I_4\to 0$.}}

elements.sent-2411-092: Finally, it could be observed that following lemma holds Lemma 2.4 \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{H\to 0^+}\left|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\right| = 2\pi. \end{aligned}\end{equation} To prove Lemma 2.3, a direct calculation using the expression of $I$ in terms of the function $F$ yields \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0) = 8[F(0,0,0)-F(0,0,H)] - 8[F(0,1,0)-F(0,1,H)] - 8[F(1,0,0)-F(1,0,H)] + 8[F(1,1,0)-F(1,1,H)] = -\frac{8}{3}H^3 + \frac{8}{3}(1-\sqrt{1+H^2}) + \frac{16}{3}H^2\sqrt{1+H^2} -12 H^2\tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+H^2}} + \frac{8}{3}(\sqrt{2+H^2}-\sqrt{2}) -\frac{8}{3}H^2\sqrt{2+H^2} + 4(\tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-\tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2+H^2}}) + 12H^2\tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2+H^2}} + 8H\tan^{-1}\frac{1}{H\sqrt{2+H^2}} \end{aligned}\end{equation} then \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{H\to 0^+}\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0) =4\lim_{H\to 0^+}\tan^{-1}\frac{1}{H\sqrt{2+H^2}} =4\cdot \frac{\pi}{2} =2\pi. \end{aligned}\end{equation} This proves Lemma 2.4.
elements.sent-2411-093: Based on Lemma 2.4, one find that for any $\epsilon>0$, there is some $H>0$ such that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Bigg|\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\bigg| - 2\pi\Bigg|<\frac{\epsilon}{3}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Secondly, based on Lemma 2.3, there is some $N\in \mathbb{N ^+$ such that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Bigg| \bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg| -\left|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\right| \Bigg| \leq\Bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0) -\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\Bigg|<\frac{\epsilon}{3}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Finally, base on Lemma 2.2, there is some $h>0$ such that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N)) -\bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg|\Bigg| <\frac{\epsilon}{3}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} By triangle inequality: \begin{equation}\notag \begin{aligned} \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N)) - 2\pi\Bigg| = \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N)) -\bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg| +\bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg| -\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\bigg| +\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\bigg|-2\pi\Bigg| \leq \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N)) -\bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg|\Bigg| +\Bigg|\bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg| -\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\bigg|\Bigg| \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} +\Bigg|\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\bigg|-2\pi\Bigg| <\frac{\epsilon}{3}+\frac{\epsilon}{3}+\frac{\epsilon}{3} =\epsilon. \end{aligned}\end{equation} This completes the proof of Lemma 2.. \nocite{*.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: For entanglement to occur, the inequality $2 \gamma_m k_B T < \hbar G \Lambda \rho$ must be satisfied, linking mechanical dissipation $\gamma_m$, effective temperature $T$, oscillator density $\rho$ and form factor $\Lambda$ determined by the geometry and spatial arrangement of the oscillators.
elements.sent-2411-095: This inequality, based on the inherent property of the noise model of GIE, is considered universally across experimental systems and cannot be improved by quantum control.
elements.sent-2411-096: In this work, we prove that the form factor $\Lambda$ has a supremum of $2\pi$, revealing a fundamental limit of the oscillator system.
elements.sent-2411-097: We propose design schemes that enable the form factor to approach this supremum, which is nearly an order of magnitude higher than typical spherical oscillators.
elements.sent-2411-098: This optimization may ease experimental constraints, bringing GIE-based validation of quantum gravity closer to realization.
elements.sent-2411-099: In recent years, advances in precision measurement technology have raised the possibility of detection of gravitational effects within massive quantum systems at lower energies in the foreseeable future \cite{Santos2017, Li2018, Westphal2021, Whittle2021, Liu2021, Youssefi2023, Fuchs2024, Bose2023, Carney2019, Gallerati2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-100: Various experimental schemes have been proposed for such studies.
elements.sent-2411-101: Across proposals like two free oscillators \cite{Kafri2014 , linearized optomechanics \cite{Miao2020 , levitated nano-systems \cite{Rijavec2021 , and modulated optomechanics \cite{Plato2023 , requirements align with the same inequality \begin{equation}\label{eq:ineq1} \begin{aligned} 2 \gamma_m k_B T < \hbar G \Lambda \rho \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\gamma_m$ is the mechanical dissipation of the oscillator, $T$ is the system effective temperature, $\rho$ is density of the oscillator, and $\Lambda$ is a form factor related to the geometry and spatial arrangements of the two oscillators.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-228: For any $x_1,y_i,z_i,x'_1,y'_i,z'_i\geq 0$ where $i=+,-$, the following sixfold parametrized definite integral can be analytically evaluated \begin{equation}\label{eq:MainIntegral} \begin{aligned} I(x_+,x_-,y_+,y_-,z_+,z_-;x'_+,x'_-,y'_+,y'_-,z'_+,z'_-) = \int_{x_-}^{x_+}\mathrm{d}x \int_{x'_-}^{x'_+}\mathrm{d}x' \int_{y_-}^{y_+}\mathrm{d}y \int_{y'_-}^{y'_+}\mathrm{d}y' \int_{z_-}^{z_+}\mathrm{d}z \int_{z'_-}^{z'_+}\mathrm{d}z' \frac{2(z-z')^2 - (x-x')^2 - (y-y')^2}{[(x-x')^2 + (y-y')^2 + (z-z')^2]^{5/2}} =\sum_{i,i',j,j',k,k'\in \{+,-\}}(-1)^{i}(-1)^{i'}(-1)^{j}(-1)^{j'}(-1)^{k}(-1)^{k'} F(x_{i}-x_{i'}',y_{j}-y_{j'}',z_{k}-z_{k'}') \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} F(x,y,z) = \lim_{(x',y',z')\to (x,y,z)}\frac{2z^2 - x^2 - y^2}{6}\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} +\frac{1}{2}x(y^2-z^2)\tanh^{-1}\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}} +\frac{1}{2}y(x^2-z^2)\tanh^{-1}\frac{y}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}} -xyz\tan^{-1}\frac{xy}{z\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} and $(-1)^\pm = \pm 1$.\newline Lemma 2.1 can be verified by applying the Newton-Leibniz formula to Eq. \ref{eq:MainIntegral .}

elements.sent-2411-235: Hence, by Lagrange's mean value theorem, one concludes that for all $1\leq i,j\leq N$, $i,j\in\mathbb{N ^+$, $\exists X_i \in (\frac{2i-2 {2N ,\frac{2i-1 {2N )$ and $\exists X'_j \in (\frac{2j-1 {2N ,\frac{2j {2N )$ such that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{4N^2}g(X_i,X'_j) = \frac{1}{4N^2}\frac{\partial^2 G_{ij}(x,x')}{\partial x\partial x'}|_{(x,x')=(X_i,X'_j)} = I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0) \end{aligned}\end{equation} and hence \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{N\to \infty}\sum_{i,j}\frac{1}{4N^2}g(X_i,X'_j) =\lim_{N\to \infty}\sum_{i,j} I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Note that $X_i \in (\frac{2i-2 {2N ,\frac{2i-1 {2N )$ and $X'_j \in (\frac{2j-1 {2N ,\frac{2j {2N )$ also implies $X_i \in (\frac{i-1 {N ,\frac{i {N )$ and $X'_j \in (\frac{j-1 {N ,\frac{j {N )$, according to the definition of Riemann integral, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{N\to \infty}\sum_{i,j}\frac{1}{4N^2}g(X_i,X'_j) =\frac{1}{4}\lim_{N\to \infty}\sum_{i,j}\frac{1}{N^2}\frac{\partial^2 G(x,x')}{\partial x\partial x'}|_{(x,x')=(X_i,X'_j)} =\frac{1}{4}G(1,1) =\frac{1}{4} I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0). \end{aligned}\end{equation} This proves Lemma 2.3.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: Finally, it could be observed that following lemma holds\newline Lemma 2.4. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{H\to 0^+}\left|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\right| = 2\pi. \end{aligned}\end{equation}\newline To prove Lemma 2.4, a direct calculation using the expression of $I$ in terms of the function $F$ yields \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0) = 8[F(0,0,0)-F(0,0,H)] - 8[F(0,1,0)-F(0,1,H)] - 8[F(1,0,0)-F(1,0,H)] + 8[F(1,1,0)-F(1,1,H)] = 8H\tan^{-1}\frac{1}{H\sqrt{2+H^2}} -\frac{8}{3}H^3 + \frac{8}{3}(1-\sqrt{1+H^2}) + \frac{8}{3}(\sqrt{2+H^2}-\sqrt{2}) +\frac{8}{3}H^2 (2\sqrt{1+H^2} -\sqrt{2+H^2}) +8H^2 (\tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2+H^2}} - \tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+H^2}}) +8(\tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2+H^2}}) \end{aligned}\end{equation} then \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{H\to 0^+}\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0) =4\lim_{H\to 0^+}\tan^{-1}\frac{1}{H\sqrt{2+H^2}} =4\cdot \frac{\pi}{2} =2\pi. \end{aligned}\end{equation} This proves Lemma 2.4.
elements.sent-2411-237: Based on Lemma 2.4, one find that for any $\epsilon>0$, there is some $H>0$ such that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Bigg|\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\bigg| - 2\pi\Bigg|<\frac{\epsilon}{3}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Secondly, based on Lemma 2.3, there is some $N\in \mathbb{N ^+$ such that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Bigg| \bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg| -\left|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\right| \Bigg| \leq\Bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0) -\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\Bigg|<\frac{\epsilon}{3}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Finally, base on Lemma 2.2, there is some $h>0$ such that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N)) -\bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg|\Bigg| <\frac{\epsilon}{3}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} By triangle inequality: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N)) - 2\pi\Bigg| = \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N)) -\bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg| +\bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg| -\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\bigg| +\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\bigg|-2\pi\Bigg| \leq \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N)) -\bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg|\Bigg| +\Bigg|\bigg|\frac{2}{H}\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0)\bigg| -\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\bigg|\Bigg| +\Bigg|\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)\bigg|-2\pi\Bigg| <\frac{\epsilon}{3}+\frac{\epsilon}{3}+\frac{\epsilon}{3} =\epsilon. \end{aligned}\end{equation} This completes the proof of Lemma 2.. \nocite{*.}}
elements.sent-2411-238: The interface between quantum mechanics and gravity remains an unresolved issue.
elements.sent-2411-239: Recent advances in precision measurement suggest that detecting gravity-induced entanglement in oscillator systems could provide key evidence for the quantum nature of gravity.

elements.sent-2411-242: Given the difficulty in further optimizing $\omega_m$, $\gamma_m$, $\rho$, and $T$, optimizing $\Lambda$ can relax the constraints on these parameters.
elements.sent-2411-243: In this work, we prove that the form factor has a supremum of $2\pi$, revealing a fundamental limit of the oscillator system.
elements.sent-2411-244: We propose designs that approach this supremum, nearly an order of magnitude higher than typical spherical oscillators.
elements.sent-2411-245: This optimization could ease experimental constraints and bring quantum gravity validation based on gravity-induced entanglement closer to realization.

elements.sent-2411-258: For any $x_i,y_i,z_i,x'_i,y'_i,z'_i\geq 0$ where $i=+,-$, the following sixfold parametrized definite integral can be analytically evaluated \begin{equation}\label{eq:MainIntegral} \begin{aligned} I(x_+,x_-,y_+,y_-,z_+,z_-;x'_+,x'_-,y'_+,y'_-,z'_+,z'_-) = \int_{x_-}^{x_+}\mathrm{d}x \int_{x'_-}^{x'_+}\mathrm{d}x' \int_{y_-}^{y_+}\mathrm{d}y \int_{y'_-}^{y'_+}\mathrm{d}y' \int_{z_-}^{z_+}\mathrm{d}z \int_{z'_-}^{z'_+}\mathrm{d}z' \frac{2(z-z')^2 - (x-x')^2 - (y-y')^2}{[(x-x')^2 + (y-y')^2 + (z-z')^2]^{5/2}} =\sum_{i,i',j,j',k,k'\in \{+,-\}}(-1)^{i}(-1)^{i'}(-1)^{j}(-1)^{j'}(-1)^{k}(-1)^{k'} F(x_{i}-x_{i'}',y_{j}-y_{j'}',z_{k}-z_{k'}') \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} F(x,y,z) = \lim_{(x',y',z')\to (x,y,z)}\frac{2z^2 - x^2 - y^2}{6}\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} +\frac{1}{2}x(y^2-z^2)\tanh^{-1}\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}} +\frac{1}{2}y(x^2-z^2)\tanh^{-1}\frac{y}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}} -xyz\tan^{-1}\frac{xy}{z\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} and $(-1)^\pm = \pm 1$.\newline Lemma 2.1 can be verified by applying the Newton-Leibniz formula to Eq. \ref{eq:MainIntegral .}

The oscillation direction is along the $z$-axis, i.e., $n = e_z$. elements.sent-2411-293: This appendix provides the rigorous mathematical proof of Theorem 1 stated in the main text.
elements.sent-2411-294: Recall that the supremum refers to a value such that the variable never exceeds it, but can reach or get arbitrarily close to it, to prove Theorem 1, the key is to show that $\Lambda (A,B,n)$ are both $\leq 2\pi$ and can be arbitrarily close to $2\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-295: They can be stated as the following two lemmas.
elements.sent-2411-296: For Lemma 1, we provide the proof in section 1 of Appendix \ref{appendix:A .}
elements.sent-2411-297: And for Lemma 2, the proof is actually already stated in the main text using the properties of Riemann integral.
elements.sent-2411-298: Here we present another more direct proof by giving a construction and evaluating the form factor explicitly in section 2 of Appendix \ref{appendix:A .}
elements.sent-2411-299: Here, we first present Lemma 1 and Lemma 2, and briefly outline the ideas of their proofs.
elements.sent-2411-300: To prove Lemma 1, according to the definition of $\Lambda$ (Eq. \ref{eq:Lambda in the main text), we only need to show that the expression inside the absolute value in Eq. \ref{eq:Lambda lies between $-2\pi$ and $2\pi$.}}
elements.sent-2411-301: We first prove, in Lemma 1.1, that this expression can be decomposed into the difference of two parts, each being a semidefinite matrix $\mathcal{M _i(i=1,2)$ with trace $2\pi$, and contracted on both sides with the unit vector $n$ representing the oscillation direction.}
elements.sent-2411-302: With this decomposition, it becomes straightforward to further prove that the expression lies within the range $-2\pi$ to $2\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-303: To prove Lemma 2, we explicitly constructed a comb-like interleaved configuration (as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:fig3 of the main text) and, through direct calculation, showed that under certain geometric limits, the form factor of this configuration can asymptotically approach $2\pi$.}
elements.sent-2411-304: The proof of Lemma 2 mainly consists of four sub-lemmas.
elements.sent-2411-305: In particular, Lemma 2.1 presents the general formula for the form factor of an oscillator composed of two rectangular blocks.
elements.sent-2411-306: Since our constructed configuration can be decomposed into multiple such blocks, we can apply Lemma 2.1 to express its form factor explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-307: Lemmas 2.2 to 2.4 sequentially address three geometric limits: the cap thickness $h$ approaching zero, the number of teeth $N$ tending to infinity, and the height $H$ approaching zero (The definitions of the these geometric parameters can be found in Fig. \ref{fig:fig3 of the main text).}
elements.sent-2411-308: Through direct calculation, we demonstrate that by applying these three limits, the form factor asymptotically approaches $2\pi$.

elements.sent-2411-327: Now we complete the proof of Lemma 1.
elements.sent-2411-328: To prove Lemma 2, we construct a specific oscillator configuration.
elements.sent-2411-329: Each oscillator consists of a number of mutually parallel rectangular plates (like teeth), which are connected by a cap to form a comb-like object.
elements.sent-2411-330: Two such oscillators are interleaved to form our system.
elements.sent-2411-331: The direction of oscillation is set to be along the $z$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-332: The shape of the oscillator is shown in Fig. \ref{fig:fig3 of the main text.}
elements.sent-2411-333: Our proof will first express the form factor of this oscillator configuration, then compute its limiting value under a series of geometric limits, demonstrating that it equals $2\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-334: The shape of the oscillators we constructed can be rigorously expressed under three-dimensional Cartesian coordinate as the following two domains, $A$ and $B$: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \overline{A(H,h,N)} = \{(x,y,z)\in \mathbb{R}^3| \frac{2i-2}{2N}\leq x\leq \frac{2i-1}{2N} \text{for some $i=1,...,N$}, 0\leq y\leq 1, 0\leq z\leq H\} \cup \{(x,y,z)\in \mathbb{R}^3| 0\leq x,y\leq 1, -h \leq z\leq 0\} \end{aligned}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \overline{B(H,h,N)} = \{(x,y,z)\in \mathbb{R}^3| \frac{2j-1}{2N}\leq x\leq \frac{2j}{2N} \text{for some $j=1,...,N$}, 0\leq y\leq 1, 0\leq z\leq H\} \cup \{(x,y,z)\in \mathbb{R}^3| 0\leq x,y\leq 1, H \leq z\leq H+h\}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here, $N$ is the number of teeth on each oscillator, $H$ is the height of each tooth, and $h$ is the thickness of the cap.
elements.sent-2411-335: Both the length and width are set to $L = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-336: You may refer directly to Fig. \ref{fig:fig3 in the main text for a visual understanding of these parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-337: Since both $A$ and $B$ are composed of multiple rectangular blocks (both the teeth and the caps), the form factor between $A$ and $B$ can be obtained by summing the contribution corresponding to each pair of rectangular blocks.
elements.sent-2411-338: Therefore, we begin by considering the form factor between an arbitrary pair of rectangular blocks.
elements.sent-2411-339: Without loss of generality, we assume the spatial domains of two rectangular objects are $[x_-, x_+] \times [y_-, y_+] \times [z_-, z_+]$ and $[x'_-, x'_+] \times [y'_-, y'_+] \times [z'_-, z'_+]$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-340: In the following Lemma 2.1, we introduce an integral $I$ which equals the integral part of the form factor between such one pair of rectangular blocks (refer to Eq. \ref{eq:Lambda in the main text, but without dividing by the volume).}
elements.sent-2411-341: The integrand in $I$ corresponds to the tidal force between a pair of unit-mass point (with the gravitational constant set to 1).
elements.sent-2411-342: The six-fold integral is taken over the spatial domains of both rectangular blocks.. Lemma 2.1.
elements.sent-2411-343: For any $x_i,y_i,z_i,x'_i,y'_i,z'_i\geq 0$ where $i=+,-$, the following sixfold parametrized definite integral can be analytically evaluated \begin{equation}\label{eq:MainIntegral} \begin{aligned} I(x_+,x_-,y_+,y_-,z_+,z_-;x'_+,x'_-,y'_+,y'_-,z'_+,z'_-) = \int_{x_-}^{x_+}\mathrm{d}x \int_{x'_-}^{x'_+}\mathrm{d}x' \int_{y_-}^{y_+}\mathrm{d}y \int_{y'_-}^{y'_+}\mathrm{d}y' \int_{z_-}^{z_+}\mathrm{d}z \int_{z'_-}^{z'_+}\mathrm{d}z' \frac{2(z-z')^2 - (x-x')^2 - (y-y')^2}{[(x-x')^2 + (y-y')^2 + (z-z')^2]^{5/2}} =\sum_{i,i',j,j',k,k'\in \{+,-\}}(-1)^{i}(-1)^{i'}(-1)^{j}(-1)^{j'}(-1)^{k}(-1)^{k'} F(x_{i}-x_{i'}',y_{j}-y_{j'}',z_{k}-z_{k'}'), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $(-1)^\pm = \pm 1$, and \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} F(x,y,z) = \lim_{(x',y',z')\to (x,y,z)}\Bigg[\frac{2z'^2 - x'^2 - y'^2}{6}\sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2 + z'^2} +\frac{1}{2}x'(y'^2-z'^2)\tanh^{-1}\frac{x'}{\sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2 + z'^2}} +\frac{1}{2}y'(x'^2-z'^2)\tanh^{-1}\frac{y'}{\sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2 + z'^2}} -x'y'z'\tan^{-1}\frac{x'y'}{z'\sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2 + z'^2}}\Bigg]. \end{aligned}\end{equation} \newline The integral in Lemma 2.1 is calculated directly by evaluating the definite integrals layer by layer.
elements.formula-2411-095: \begin{equation} F(x,y,z) = \lim_{(x',y',z')\to (x,y,z)}\Bigg[\frac{2z'^2 - x'^2 - y'^2}{6}\sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2 + z'^2} +\frac{1}{2}x'(y'^2-z'^2)\tanh^{-1}\frac{x'}{\sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2 + z'^2}} +\frac{1}{2}y'(x'^2-z'^2)\tanh^{-1}\frac{y'}{\sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2 + z'^2}} -x'y'z'\tan^{-1}\frac{x'y'}{z'\sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2 + z'^2}}\Bigg]. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-344: One can verify this formula by taking multiple derivatives of the result and checking for consistency.
elements.sent-2411-345: Taking the limit in the expression of $F(x,y,z)$ is to ensure that the expression is well-defined, even in special cases when some denominators tend to zero.
elements.sent-2411-346: With the help of Lemma 2.1, for any $H, h>0$ and $N \in \mathbb{N ^+$, one could express the form factor $\Lambda$ of the two comb-like oscillators $A(H,h,N)$ and $B(H,h,N)$ and direction $n =e_z$ in terms of the integral $I$, given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:form_lemma2} \begin{aligned} \Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N),e_z) = \left|\frac{2}{H+2h}\left[I_1(H,N) + I_2(H,h,N) + I_3(H,h,N) + I_4(H,h)\right]\right|, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} I_1(H,N) =\sum_{i,j}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0), I_2(H,h,N) =\sum_{i}^{N}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H+h,H), I_3(H,h,N)=\sum_{j}^{N}I(\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,0,-h), I_4(H,h)=I(1,0,1,0,H+h,H;1,0,1,0,0,-h). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here, the integral $I_1$ corresponds to the gravitational contribution between the teeth of the two oscillators; integrals $I_2$ and $I_3$ correspond to the gravitational interactions between the teeth of one oscillator and the cap of the other; and $I_4$ accounts for the interaction between the two caps.}
elements.sent-2411-347: The prefactor $2 / (H + 2h)$ is the reciprocal of the volume of one oscillator (recall that we have already set $L=1$), matching the $1/V$ factor in Eq. \ref{eq:Lambda of the main text.}
elements.sent-2411-348: Next, we will take certain limits to verify that the form factor in Eq. \ref{eq:form_lemma2 can approach $2\pi$ arbitrarily closely.}
elements.sent-2411-349: Firstly, fixing any $H$ and $N$, one finds that while $h\to 0$, $I_2, I_3, I_4\to 0$ and only $I_1$ remains.
elements.sent-2411-351: This limit corresponds to the case where the cap becomes infinitesimally thin.
elements.sent-2411-352: This conclusion is quite intuitive, since the integrals $I_2$, $I_3$, and $I_4$ all represent the gravitational contributions that involve the caps.. \newline Lemma 2.2.
elements.sent-2411-353: For any $H>0$ and $N\in\mathbb{N ^+$, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{h\to 0^+}\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N),e_z)= \left|\frac{2}{H}I_1(H,N)\right|. \end{aligned}\end{equation} To rigorously prove Lemma 2.2, it can be seen that $I$ is continuous with respect to its parameters, since $F$ is continuous.}
elements.sent-2411-354: This gives \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{h\to 0^+}I_2(N,h,N) = \lim_{h\to 0^+}\sum_{i}^{N}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H+h,H) = \sum_{i}^{N}\lim_{h\to 0^+}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H+h,H) = \sum_{i}^{N}\lim_{h\to 0^+}I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,H) =0. \end{aligned}\end{equation} For $I_3$ and $I_4$, $\lim_{h\to 0^+ I_{3 (H,h,N)= \lim_{h\to 0^+ I_{4 (H,h) = 0$ can be proved similarly.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-355: The following lemma holds, thus the expression in Lemma 2.2 can be further simplified.. \newline Lemma 2.3.
elements.sent-2411-356: For any $H >0$, define $I_5(H)=I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)$, then \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{N\to \infty}\sum_{i,j}I_1(H,h,N)=\frac{1}{4}I_5(H). \end{aligned}\end{equation}\newline To prove Lemma 2.3, we define the following functions \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} G(x,x') = I(x,0,1,0,H,0;x',0,1,0,H,0), G_{ij}(x,x') = I(x,\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;x',\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0), g(x,x') = \frac{\partial^2 G(x,x')}{\partial x\partial x'} =\frac{\partial^2 G_{ij}(x,x')}{\partial x\partial x'}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} It can be observed that for all $1\leq i,j\leq N$, $i,j\in\mathbb{N ^+$, the function $G_{ij}$ is continuous on $[\frac{2i-2 {2N ,\frac{2i-1 {2N ]\times [\frac{2j-1 {2N ,\frac{2j {2N ]$ and is differentiable on $(\frac{2i-2 {2N ,\frac{2i-1 {2N )\times (\frac{2j-1 {2N ,\frac{2j {2N )$ by Lemma 2.1.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-357: Hence, by Lagrange's mean value theorem, one concludes that for all $1\leq i,j\leq N$, $i,j\in\mathbb{N ^+$, $\exists X_i \in (\frac{2i-2 {2N ,\frac{2i-1 {2N )$ and $\exists X'_j \in (\frac{2j-1 {2N ,\frac{2j {2N )$ such that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{4N^2}g(X_i,X'_j) = \frac{1}{4N^2}\frac{\partial^2 G_{ij}(x,x')}{\partial x\partial x'}|_{(x,x')=(X_i,X'_j)} = I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0) \end{aligned}\end{equation} and hence \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{N\to \infty}\sum_{i,j}\frac{1}{4N^2}g(X_i,X'_j) =\lim_{N\to \infty}\sum_{i,j} I(\frac{2i-1}{2N},\frac{2i-2}{2N},1,0,H,0;\frac{2j}{2N},\frac{2j-1}{2N},1,0,H,0). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Note that $X_i \in (\frac{2i-2 {2N ,\frac{2i-1 {2N )$ and $X'_j \in (\frac{2j-1 {2N ,\frac{2j {2N )$ also implies $X_i \in (\frac{i-1 {N ,\frac{i {N )$ and $X'_j \in (\frac{j-1 {N ,\frac{j {N )$, according to the definition of Riemann integral, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{N\to \infty}\sum_{i,j}\frac{1}{4N^2}g(X_i,X'_j) =\frac{1}{4}\lim_{N\to \infty}\sum_{i,j}\frac{1}{N^2}\frac{\partial^2 G(x,x')}{\partial x\partial x'}|_{(x,x')=(X_i,X'_j)} =\frac{1}{4}G(1,1) =\frac{1}{4} I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0)=\frac{1}{4}I_5(H). \end{aligned}\end{equation} This proves Lemma 2.3.\newline Finally, the last limit we take is to let the height of the teeth tend to zero.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-358: It could be observed that the following lemma holds, and leads us to our final conclusion \newline Lemma 2.4. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{H\to 0^+}\left|\frac{1}{2H}I_5(H)\right| = 2\pi. \end{aligned}\end{equation}\newline To prove Lemma 2.4, a direct calculation using the expression of $I$ in terms of the function $F$ yields \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} I_5(H) =I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0) = 8[F(0,0,0)-F(0,0,H)] - 8[F(0,1,0)-F(0,1,H)] - 8[F(1,0,0)-F(1,0,H)] + 8[F(1,1,0)-F(1,1,H)] = 8H\tan^{-1}\frac{1}{H\sqrt{2+H^2}} -\frac{8}{3}H^3 + \frac{8}{3}(1-\sqrt{1+H^2}) + \frac{8}{3}(\sqrt{2+H^2}-\sqrt{2}) +\frac{8}{3}H^2 (2\sqrt{1+H^2} -\sqrt{2+H^2}) +8H^2 (\tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2+H^2}} - \tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+H^2}}) +8(\tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \tanh^{-1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2+H^2}}), \end{aligned}\end{equation} then \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{H\to 0^+}\frac{1}{2H}I_5(H) =\lim_{H\to 0^+}\frac{1}{2H}I(1,0,1,0,H,0;1,0,1,0,H,0) =4\lim_{H\to 0^+}\tan^{-1}\frac{1}{H\sqrt{2+H^2}} =4\cdot \frac{\pi}{2} =2\pi. \end{aligned}\end{equation} This proves Lemma 2.4.
elements.sent-2411-359: Based on Lemma 2.4, for any $\epsilon>0$, there is some $H>0$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:ineq1} \begin{aligned} \Bigg|\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I_5(H)\bigg| - 2\pi\Bigg|<\frac{\epsilon}{3}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} And based on Lemma 2.3, there is some $N\in \mathbb{N ^+$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:ineq2} \begin{aligned} \Bigg| \bigg|\frac{2}{H}I_1(H,N)\bigg|-\left|\frac{1}{2H}I_5(H)\right| \Bigg|\leq \Bigg|\frac{2}{H}I_1(H,N)-\frac{1} {2H}I_5(H)\Bigg| < \frac{\epsilon}{3}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Finally, based on Lemma 2.2, there is some $h>0$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:ineq3} \begin{aligned} \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N),e_z)-\bigg|\frac{2}{H}I_1(H,N)\bigg|\Bigg|<\frac{\epsilon}{3}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} By triangle inequality and Eq. \ref{eq:ineq1 -\ref{eq:ineq3 , we arrive at the conclusion that for any $\varepsilon$, there exist some $H$, $h$, and $N$ such that \begin{equation}\notag \begin{aligned} \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N),e_z) - 2\pi\Bigg| = \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N),e_z))-\bigg|\frac{2}{H}I_1(H,N)\bigg| +\bigg|\frac{2}{H}I_1(H,N)\bigg|-\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I_5(H)\bigg|+\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I_5(H)\bigg|-2\pi\Bigg| \leq \Bigg|\Lambda(A(H,h,N),B(H,h,N),e_z))-\bigg|\frac{2}{H}I_1(H,N)\bigg|\Bigg| +\Bigg|\bigg|\frac{2}{H}I_1(H,N)\bigg|-\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I_5(H)\bigg|\Bigg|+\Bigg|\bigg|\frac{1}{2H}I_5(H)\bigg|-2\pi\Bigg| <\frac{\epsilon}{3}+\frac{\epsilon}{3}+\frac{\epsilon}{3} =\epsilon. \end{aligned}\end{equation} This completes the proof of Lemma 2.. \nocite{*.}}}}
